issues.body: Every reference in the corpus is a syntactically valid RFC 3986 reference, so oxiri_base.resolve(reference) never actually returns Err today and the `let Ok(expected) = ... else { continue };` guard never fires. That is fine now, but it means a future oxiri upgrade (or an accidental corpus edit introducing an invalid reference) could cause cases to be skipped silently, potentially reducing the differential test to zero comparisons with no signal — the exact failure mode a tripwire is meant to prevent.
issues.fix_hint: Since the corpus is hand-picked and all entries are valid, use `.expect("oxiri should resolve <base>/<reference>")` (or assert on a minimum number of executed comparisons via a counter) instead of `continue`, so a silent-skip regression turns the test red rather than passing vacuously.
issues.title: `else { continue }` can silently make the tripwire vacuous

verdict.summary: Test-only change (issue #3714) adding a differential 'mutation tripwire' that compares the crate's dependency-free RFC 3986 reference resolver (resolve_iri) against oxiri across the RFC 3986 §5.4 normal/abnormal corpus plus existing JSON-LD @base shapes. oxiri is added strictly under [dev-dependencies] (oxiri = "0.2", locked to 0.2.11), so the crate's advertised zero-mandatory/wasm-lean design is preserved; Cargo.lock reflects only the dev edge. The test has real assertions (assert_eq! resolve_iri(base,ref) == oxiri.resolve(ref).into_inner()) with an informative failure message and is not vacuous. I traced multiple corpus cases against the implementation's split_uri/merge/remove_dot_segments/recompose transform (?y, empty ref, #s, g.., http:g, empty-base-path merge for 'https://example.com'+'relative') and all agree with oxiri's strict RFC 3986 output, so the test locks in currently-correct behaviour. The oxiri 0.2 API used (Iri::parse -> resolve -> into_inner) matches the shipped 0.2.11 surface and returns String, matching resolve_iri's return type, so it compiles. Correct and complete; approved. One non-blocking nit below.
